MMRW-T5K: Major Thomas Owsley I was born in 1658 in Stogursey, Somerset, England, and crossed the Atlantic to Virginia before September 1677. His passage was marked by drama: in 1679 he was captured by Algerian pirates and held until he was ransomed. Settling in colonial Virginia, he rose to prominence as a militia officer, holding the titles of Captain and Major, and served as Sheriff of Stafford. He died in 1700 at Fort Belvoir in Fairfax County.
LK2S-TTV: Capt. William Sorrell Ballard Sr. was born in 1682 in Virginia and lived his life in the colony's Tidewater country. His name appears in the Virginia public records of James City County in March 1702 in connection with military service, and over the years he made his home in James City and King and Queen counties, as well as at Scott's Landing in Albemarle. He died in 1754 in King and Queen, Virginia.
LT1V-G6V: David Cargill Sr. was born in 1661 in Perth, Scotland, and as a young man around 1680 joined the Scots migration to Derry, Ireland, where he served as a Ruling Elder in the Presbyterian Church at Aghadowey. In August 1718 he sailed aboard the "Robert" to Boston with his daughter Annis and four grandchildren, part of the Scots-Irish settlement of New England. Known as Captain Cargill, he operated a grist and fulling mill and was elected Moderator and First Selectman from 1726 to 1728. He settled at Londonderry, New Hampshire, where he died in 1734, his will probated that May with his son David as executor.
KF51-SDQ: Pierre Cresson was born in 1609 in Sedan, France, a Protestant of the Huguenot faith. While living in Holland he worked around 1650 as a gardener for the Prince of Orange. In 1657 he answered Amsterdam's call for settlers and sailed to New Amstel, the Dutch colony on the Delaware River. He later settled on Staten Island, where in 1679 French-speaking missionaries recorded visiting him at his home on Long Creek by Fresh Kill. He died there in 1682.
KZNY-PF7: Peter Larsson Kock was born in 1610 in Södermanland, Sweden, and arrived in the Delaware region by 1641 as part of the early Swedish colonial settlement. He served as a soldier and later as a magistrate and justice in the colony along the Delaware. He made his will in June 1687 and died that year, his estate later settled at Philadelphia.
LHKZ-M4Z: John Stone Sr. was born in 1618 in Nayland, Suffolk, England, and made his way to Massachusetts Bay Colony, settling at Cambridge. He wrote his will in February 1683 and died that same year.
LBY4-C5N: Nicholas Gentry I was born about 1655 in England and around 1675 followed his older brother Samuel to the Virginia Colony, where he arrived as an indentured servant. He spent his life in New Kent County, recorded on the 1704 Rent Roll as a tenant on 250 acres of Crown land, possibly working the same ground as his brother. He died in New Kent in 1709.
LZG3-3BW: William Basse was born in 1654 in Isle of Wight County, Virginia. In 1727 he found it necessary to formally prove that he was of Native American rather than African descent, at a time when any record of African ancestry could cost a family its rights and freedom. He made his will in October 1740 and died in Norfolk County in 1741, his will proved in 1742.
LJYD-THT: Col. John Catlett IV was born in 1626 in Kent, England, and immigrated to Virginia in 1650. He became a substantial landholder in the Rappahannock country, patenting hundreds of acres along the river and Occupason Creek through the 1650s and 1660s, often in partnership with Ralph Rousey. He rose to the rank of Colonel in the colonial militia and was killed in action in April 1670 at Port Royal, Caroline County, Virginia.
LZ68-2X1: Ann Almy was born in 1627 in South Kilworth, Leicestershire, England. In 1635 she emigrated from London aboard the ship "Abigail" to British Colonial America. She lived to the age of about eighty-two and died in 1709 at Warwick, in the Rhode Island colony.
LT5R-K32: John Stockbridge was born in 1608 in Rayleigh, Essex, England, and worked as a wheelwright. In June 1635 he sailed from London aboard the ship "Blessing" to the Massachusetts Bay Colony, and by 1638 was living in Scituate in the Plymouth Colony. That same year he ran afoul of the colony's authorities, being fined for what the court called disgraceful and jeering speeches against the government. He later resided in Boston and died there in 1657.
KNCK-NX8: Anne Rogers was born in 1615 and lived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony as the wife of John Stone Sr. She was still living in February 1683 when her husband made his will. She died that same year, leaving no known headstone or recorded burial place.
L5TJ-17K: John How was born in 1620 in England and had settled at Sudbury, Massachusetts, before 1640. Around 1659 he moved west to become the first English settler of Marlborough, and about 1661 he opened the town's first public house. He also worked as a glover. He made his will in May 1680 and died that year at Marlborough.
LB96-WGG: Gedeon La Plante Merlet was born in 1624 in the Dordogne region of France, a French Huguenot. By 1644 he was working as a carpenter in the Netherlands, living on the Santstraet in Rotterdam. In 1663 he immigrated to Staten Island in New Amsterdam, the colony that would become New York. He died in 1683 at Piscataway, New Jersey.
93WR-TBF: Elizabeth Hull was born in 1627 in Northleigh, Devon, England, and in 1635 immigrated from Weymouth, England, to the Massachusetts Bay Colony. Over her long life she lived in Maine and New Hampshire, including at Kittery and Dover Point, and was the mother of fifteen children. She died in 1706 at Dover, New Hampshire.
LCJM-K4K: Isaac Robinson was born in 1610 in Leiden, Holland, among the English Separatist community there. He crossed to British Colonial America, arriving by 1631 and settling on Cape Cod, where he served from 1645 to 1651 as Barnstable's deputy to the Plymouth Colony Court and later, from 1663 to 1673, kept an inn at Falmouth. A man of conscience, he was disfranchised for thirteen years for befriending the Quakers during their persecution. He lived to a remarkable age and died in 1704 at Barnstable.
LZDP-2YN: Thomas Newhall Sr. was born in 1594 in Sherington, Buckinghamshire, England. In 1630 he immigrated with his brother Anthony to Salem in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. He settled at Lynn, Essex County, where he wrote his will in 1668 and died in 1674.
LD9G-684: Augustine Herman was born in 1605 in Mšeno, Bohemia, and became a noted cartographer. He settled in the colonies and by 1661 was at Baltimore, Maryland, later establishing himself at Bohemia Manor in Cecil County, where he died in 1686. His will was proven in 1688.
LCMX-VZC: Joane Tattershall was born in 1598 in Stapleford, Wiltshire, England. In April 1635 she left Southampton aboard the ship "James of London," arriving in Boston that June. She died in 1638.
9HJC-Z1K: Elizabeth Philbrick was born in 1621 in Bures, Suffolk, England, and in 1630 immigrated to the Massachusetts Bay Colony, with her family settling at Hampton. She appeared in court at Hampton in October 1674 and died there in 1677.
LHQ5-9KG: Major Daniel Whitehead Jr. was born in 1646 in Jamaica, Queens, in the New York Colony, where he lived his whole life and held the militia rank of Major. He made his will in November 1703 and died the following year in the town of his birth.
M2PN-PN6: Sir Hugh Owen, 1st Baronet, was born in 1604 in Hundleton, Pembrokeshire, Wales. He was created a Baronet on 11 August 1641. He died in 1670 at Saint Nicholas, Pembrokeshire, his will being recorded years afterward in 1680.
L6GZ-KHD: Edward "Ned" Garrett Jr. was born in 1733 in New Kent County, Virginia. In 1767 he received a land grant in the South Carolina backcountry of District 96, settling there and later in the Spartanburg area. He served in the military during the Revolutionary era and is recognized as a patriot by the Sons of the American Revolution. He died in 1794 in Laurens, South Carolina.
LJRD-S5D: Richard Haven was born in 1616 in Sherington, Buckinghamshire, England. He came from the west of England and had settled at Lynn, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, by 1645. He lived a long life and died at Lynn in 1703.
LVPS-64D: Thomas McGeehee was born in 1645 in Stirlingshire, Scotland, and immigrated to Virginia by the spring of 1652, taking an Oath of Allegiance that April. He settled first in York County, where in 1658 he testified in a court case and gave his age as forty, and later lived in New Kent and King William counties. He made his last will, recorded under the name Thomas Mack Gehee, in July 1727, the year of his death.
LBK9-RLM: Lt. William Harris was born in 1633 in England and arrived in Virginia in 1643. He served as a lieutenant and officer in the British Army. He settled in Virginia and died in 1698 at Lorton, in Fairfax County.
MNN8-VZT: Christopher Webb was born in 1599 in Crediton, Devon, England, and worked as a millwright. In 1629 he immigrated to the Massachusetts Bay Colony, settling at Braintree, where in 1655 he served as town clerk. He died at Braintree in 1671.
L51W-ZTP: Richard Singletary was born in 1585 in Lincolnshire, England, and arrived at Salem in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1637, settling in the colony by the following year. He lived to an extraordinary age, dying in 1687 at Haverhill, Essex County, Massachusetts, at over one hundred years old.
